items: Schedule a vet appointment for urinalysis and bloodwork within the next week to rule out urinary tract infection, kidney disease, and diabetes.
Tell your veterinarian that your adult cat has started urinating outside the litter box and request specific tests for medical causes.
If you own a male cat, mention any straining to urinate, crying during urination, or producing only small urine amounts as emergency symptoms.
Budget $100-$300 for the vet visit to rule out dangerous medical conditions before assuming this is a behavioral problem.
Count your current litter boxes and add one more box than the number of cats you own to follow the N+1 rule for proper territory coverage.
Replace any covered litter boxes with uncovered ones since covered boxes trap ammonia fumes and allow ambush opportunities.
Measure your cat from nose to base of tail and buy a litter box that is at least 1.5 times that length for adequate sizing.
Fill each box with 2 inches of unscented clumping litter, scoop it daily, and perform a full litter change every week to keep boxes clean.
Move all litter boxes away from your cat's food bowls, loud appliances, high-traffic hallways, and windows where outdoor animals might be visible.
Plug a Feliway diffuser into the room where your cat spends the most time and leave it running continuously for 2-3 weeks before evaluating results.
Add vertical space throughout your home using cat trees, shelves, or window perches so your cat can climb to feel safer and more secure.
Play with your cat for 10-15 minutes daily using interactive toys like feather wands or laser pointers to reduce stress and anxiety.
